quiz_questions.question_text: What is the component of weight parallel to an incline?

options.text: mg cos θ
mg/sin θ
mg sin θ
mg tan θ

quiz_questions.explanation: Inclined Planes Decompose weight into two components: parallel (mg sin θ, down the slope) and perpendicular (mg cos θ, into the surface). The other options — 'mg cos θ', 'mg tan θ', and 'mg/sin θ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force on an inclined plane?

options.text: N = mg sin θ
N = mg cos θ
N = mg tan θ
N = mg

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'N = mg cos θ'.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a frictionless incline, what is the acceleration?

options.text: g tan θ
g sin θ
g cos θ
g

quiz_questions.explanation: On a frictionless incline, only the parallel component of gravity accelerates the object down the slope. The acceleration is a = g sin θ. At θ = 0° (flat), a = 0. At θ = 90° (vertical), a = g.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the angle of repose?

options.text: The steepest angle at which an object stays stationary
The angle of a ramp in a lab
45 degrees
The angle at which friction disappears

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Angles The angle of repose is the steepest angle at which an object stays stationary: tan θ = μₛ. The other options — 'The angle of a ramp in a lab', '45 degrees', and 'The angle at which friction disappears' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the normal force always equal mg?

options.text: Yes, always
Only in free fall
Only on inclines
No — only on flat horizontal surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'No — only on flat horizontal surfaces'.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do we decompose forces on an inclined plane?

options.text: To make the math harder
To eliminate gravity
To separate motion along the slope from motion perpendicular to it
Forces cannot act on inclines

quiz_questions.explanation: Inclined Planes Decompose weight into two components: parallel (mg sin θ, down the slope) and perpendicular (mg cos θ, into the surface). This separates the problem into two independent directions for easier analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a steeper incline, the component of gravity along the slope:

options.text: Increases
Becomes zero
Stays the same
Decreases

quiz_questions.explanation: The parallel component of gravity is mg sin θ. As the angle θ increases (steeper slope), sin θ increases, so the component of gravity along the slope increases. At 90° the full weight acts along the slope.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a steeper incline, what happens to the normal force?

options.text: It increases as the incline gets steeper
It decreases as the incline gets steeper
It stays the same regardless of the angle
It doubles when the angle is doubled

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force on an incline is N = mg cos θ. As the angle θ increases, cos θ decreases, so the normal force gets smaller. At 90° (vertical), cos 90° = 0, so the normal force would be zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force on a flat horizontal surface for a 5 kg object? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 5 N

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat horizontal surface, the normal force equals the weight: N = mg = 5 kg × 10 m/s² = 50 N. The surface pushes up with exactly enough force to support the object's weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: At what angle does a frictionless incline give an acceleration equal to g (free fall)?

options.text: 0 degrees (flat surface)
45 degrees
90 degrees (vertical)
30 degrees

quiz_questions.explanation: The acceleration on a frictionless incline is a = g sin θ. When θ = 90°, sin 90° = 1, so a = g. This makes sense — a vertical surface is essentially free fall. At 0° (flat), sin 0° = 0 and there is no acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'normal force'?

options.text: The perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object touching it
The force of gravity pulling an object straight down toward Earth's center
The frictional resistance between two surfaces sliding against each other
The applied force that a person uses to push an object up a ramp

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force that a surface exerts perpendicular to itself on any object resting on or pressing against it. The word 'normal' in physics means 'perpendicular.' It prevents objects from passing through solid surfaces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'inclined plane' mean?

options.text: A flat surface tilted at an angle to the horizontal ground
A vertical wall that objects can be pushed against firmly
A curved ramp that changes angle along its entire length
A horizontal surface that has friction on only one side

quiz_questions.explanation: An inclined plane is a flat surface that is tilted at an angle relative to horizontal. It is one of the six simple machines. Ramps, hills, and slopes are all examples of inclined planes used in everyday life.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the word 'normal' mean in the term 'normal force'?

options.text: Typical or average in size compared to all other forces present
Perpendicular to the surface of contact between two objects
Parallel to the direction of the object's current movement
Equal in value to the weight of the object at all times

quiz_questions.explanation: In physics and mathematics, 'normal' means perpendicular (at a 90° angle). The normal force acts perpendicular to the surface. On a flat floor, it points straight up. On an inclined plane, it points away from the surface at a right angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'decompose' mean when referring to forces on an incline?

options.text: To break a force into components along and perpendicular to the slope
To remove forces from a diagram so fewer arrows are shown
To combine all forces into one single resultant force vector
To eliminate friction from the problem by making the surface smooth

quiz_questions.explanation: Decomposing a force means breaking it into two perpendicular components. On an inclined plane, we decompose the weight (mg) into a parallel component (mg sin θ along the slope) and a perpendicular component (mg cos θ into the surface).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'angle of repose' refer to?

options.text: The maximum angle at which an object can stay on a slope without sliding
The angle at which a thrown ball reaches its highest point in the air
The angle between the normal force and the gravitational force vector
The angle that gives the maximum distance for a projectile launched up

quiz_questions.explanation: The angle of repose is the steepest angle of an incline at which an object remains stationary without sliding. Beyond this angle, the component of gravity along the slope exceeds the maximum static friction, and the object slides. It is related to μₛ by tan θ = μₛ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'perpendicular component' mean on an inclined plane?

options.text: The part of the weight that presses into the surface of the incline
The part of the weight that pulls the object down along the slope
The friction force that acts along the surface of the inclined plane
The applied force that pushes the object up the inclined plane

quiz_questions.explanation: The perpendicular component of weight on an incline is the part that pushes into the surface, equal to mg cos θ. This component is balanced by the normal force. It does not cause motion along the slope but determines how hard the object presses against the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'parallel component' mean on an inclined plane?

options.text: The part of the weight that acts along the slope and causes sliding
The part of the weight that presses the object into the incline surface
The normal force that the incline exerts on the object's bottom surface
The applied force that a person exerts to push an object up the slope

quiz_questions.explanation: The parallel component of weight on an incline is mg sin θ. It acts along the slope, pulling the object downhill. This is the component responsible for causing the object to slide down the incline if friction is insufficient to hold it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'frictionless surface' mean in physics problems?

options.text: An idealized surface with zero friction, used to simplify calculations
A surface made of a special material that generates no normal force
A surface that is perfectly flat with no incline angle whatsoever
A surface where objects cannot move because friction is infinite

quiz_questions.explanation: A frictionless surface is an idealization used in physics problems where the coefficient of friction is assumed to be zero. This simplifies calculations by removing friction from the problem, allowing students to focus on other forces like gravity and normal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'weight component' refer to?

options.text: A portion of the gravitational force resolved along a specific direction
The total weight of the object measured on a standard bathroom scale
A force created by the surface to prevent the object from falling through
The difference between the weight on Earth and the weight on the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: A weight component is the portion of an object's weight (gravitational force) resolved in a particular direction. On an incline, weight is decomposed into a parallel component (mg sin θ) and a perpendicular component (mg cos θ).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'slope angle' (θ) represent on an inclined plane?

options.text: The angle between the inclined surface and the horizontal ground
The angle between the normal force and the friction force vectors
The angle at which the object rotates as it slides down the ramp
The angle between the applied force and the weight of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope angle θ is measured between the surface of the inclined plane and the horizontal ground. A larger angle means a steeper slope. At θ = 0°, the surface is flat. At θ = 90°, the surface is vertical.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a 4 kg block on a frictionless ramp tilted at 30°. What is the acceleration of the block down the ramp? (Use g = 10 m/s², sin 30° = 0.5)

options.text: 10 m/s²
5 m/s²
8.7 m/s²
2.5 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: On a frictionless incline, a = g sin θ = 10 × sin 30° = 10 × 0.5 = 5 m/s². The mass cancels out — all objects on a frictionless incline at the same angle have the same acceleration, regardless of mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the normal force on a 10 kg block sitting on a ramp tilted at 60°. What is the normal force? (Use g = 10 m/s², cos 60° = 0.5)
quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force on an incline is N = mg cos θ = 10 kg × 10 m/s² × cos 60° = 100 × 0.5 = 50 N. The steeper the angle, the smaller the normal force becomes because less of the weight pushes into the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that a toy car rolls faster down a steep ramp than a gentle ramp. What is the physics explanation?

options.text: The steeper ramp has more gravity because it is closer to vertical
A steeper angle increases the parallel component of gravity along the slope
The gentle ramp has more air resistance slowing the car down significantly
The normal force is greater on the steep ramp and pushes the car faster

quiz_questions.explanation: The parallel component of gravity is mg sin θ. A steeper ramp has a larger angle θ, which increases sin θ, making the force pulling the car down the slope larger. This produces a greater acceleration. The normal force actually decreases on steeper ramps.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student stacks books and tilts them to create a ramp. A block begins to slide when the angle reaches 25°. What does this tell the student about the coefficient of static friction?

options.text: The coefficient of static friction equals sin 25° divided by cos 25° (tan 25°)
The coefficient of static friction equals cos 25° divided by sin 25°
The coefficient of static friction equals sin 25° times cos 25°
The coefficient of static friction equals 25 divided by 90 degrees

quiz_questions.explanation: At the angle of repose, the object is about to slide, so maximum static friction equals the parallel component of gravity: μₛ mg cos θ = mg sin θ. Simplifying: μₛ = sin θ / cos θ = tan θ. So μₛ = tan 25° ≈ 0.47.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student pushes a 6 kg box up a frictionless ramp tilted at 30° at constant speed. What applied force along the ramp is needed? (Use g = 10 m/s², sin 30° = 0.5)

options.text: 60 N up the ramp
30 N up the ramp
52 N up the ramp
15 N up the ramp

quiz_questions.explanation: At constant speed (zero acceleration), the applied force must balance the parallel component of gravity: F = mg sin θ = 6 × 10 × 0.5 = 30 N up the ramp. The net force along the slope is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: A delivery worker uses a ramp to load a heavy box onto a truck instead of lifting it straight up. Why is the ramp easier to use?

options.text: The ramp eliminates gravity so the box becomes completely weightless
The ramp reduces the force needed because only mg sin θ acts along the slope
The ramp doubles the normal force which helps push the box upward faster
The ramp changes the mass of the box so it requires less energy to move

quiz_questions.explanation: A ramp reduces the force required to move an object upward because you only need to overcome mg sin θ (the parallel component of gravity) instead of the full weight mg. A gentler slope means a smaller angle and less force needed, though you push over a longer distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes that a ball placed on a flat table does not roll, but the same ball rolls immediately when placed on a tilted board. What force causes the ball to roll on the tilted board?

options.text: The normal force from the tilted board pushes the ball downhill along the surface
The parallel component of gravity (mg sin θ) pulls the ball down the slope
Air currents near the tilted board push the ball in the downhill direction
Friction on the tilted board is greater and pushes the ball down the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat surface, the parallel component of gravity is zero (sin 0° = 0), so there is no force to make the ball roll. On a tilted surface, the parallel component mg sin θ pulls the ball downhill along the slope, causing it to roll.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a 2 kg block on a ramp with friction. The ramp is at 30° and the block stays still. Is the friction force equal to mg sin 30°? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: Yes — static friction matches the parallel gravity component to keep the block still
No — friction is always equal to the full weight of the block on any surface
No — friction on a ramp always equals the normal force times the slope angle
Yes — but only because the block is exactly 2 kg on this specific ramp angle

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the block is stationary, static friction exactly balances the parallel component of gravity: fₛ = mg sin 30° = 2 × 10 × 0.5 = 10 N up the ramp. Static friction adjusts to prevent motion, up to its maximum value.

quiz_questions.question_text: A skier goes down a steep mountain slope. As the slope becomes less steep toward the bottom, the skier notices the acceleration decreasing. Why does this happen?

options.text: The skier gains mass as they move down the mountain to the valley below
Gravity becomes weaker at lower altitudes closer to sea level on Earth
A smaller slope angle means a smaller parallel gravity component (mg sin θ)
Snow at the bottom is always colder and has much more friction than above

quiz_questions.explanation: The acceleration down a slope depends on the angle: a = g sin θ (ignoring friction). As the slope becomes less steep (smaller θ), sin θ decreases and the parallel component of gravity decreases. This reduces the net force along the slope, resulting in less acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sets up an experiment where a block slides down a ramp with friction. The block moves at constant velocity. What must be true about the forces?

options.text: The parallel gravity component is greater than the kinetic friction force
The kinetic friction force exactly equals the parallel gravity component
The normal force is equal to zero because the block is on an incline
There is no friction on the ramp because the block is already sliding

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, so the net force along the slope is zero. The kinetic friction force (acting up the slope) must exactly equal the parallel component of gravity (mg sin θ, acting down the slope). The forces are balanced.
